naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the Pierpont branch through mother Mary Elizabeth Blake's ancestry (H00388 → HD1556 → HD1565).

notable.notable_blurb: Set the founding of Alcoholics Anonymous in motion

narrative_blocks.header: "Rowland H." in the AA Big Book
narrative_blocks.detail: Jung's verdict led Rowland to the Oxford Group, a Christian organization. In 1934 he arranged the release of Ebby Thacher from commitment in Vermont. Ebby then carried the Oxford Group message to Bill Wilson in Brooklyn, directly setting in motion the founding of AA. Rowland appears as "Rowland H." in the AA Big Book (p.26). He never joined AA himself. His papers are at the Rhode Island Historical Society (MSS 483) and the Beinecke Library at Yale. He served in the RI State Senate 1914-1916, and as a WWI captain in the US Army Chemical Warfare Service. Taft School; Yale 1903 (Elihu Club). (AA Big Book; AA history; Yale Beinecke; Rhode Island Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: Rowland Hazard III ("Roy") struggled with alcoholism for years. In 1926 he consulted Carl Jung in Zurich, who told him only a vital spiritual experience could help — a diagnosis that set off a chain of events leading directly to the founding of Alcoholics Anonymous.

narrative_blocks.header: Carl Jung told him medicine couldn’t help
narrative_blocks.body: Jung’s 1926 verdict — that only a vital spiritual experience could save him — sent Hazard to the Oxford Group. When he later arranged Ebby Thacher’s release from a Vermont commitment, Thacher carried that message to Bill Wilson, and Alcoholics Anonymous followed; Hazard himself never joined.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named "Pierrepont" honoring the family name of his mother Mary Pierrepont Bushnell Hazard (HD1577).

narrative_blocks.detail: St. George's School Newport RI; Yale B.A. 1915 (Wolf's Head). WWI service included Mexican border intervention. Solvay Process Company 1915-1923 (rising to assistant to the president). RI State Senator 1930-1932; RI State Treasurer 1938-1940. WWII: Chief, New England War Production Board priorities section 1941-1943; Civil Affairs Officer in Tunisia, Palermo, and Naples; Liaison Officer to Chief Quartermaster, US Army — played a major role in food distribution to liberated northwestern Europe. Discharged Lt. Colonel November 1945. Published "Civil Affairs: An Experiment in Reconstruction" (1949). His papers (20 linear feet, 1893-1979) are at the Rhode Island Historical Society (RIHS MSS 483 sg 9). (Rhode Island Historical Society; Yale; FindAGrave)
narrative_blocks.body: Served as Rhode Island State Treasurer 1938-1940; ran as Republican nominee for US Senate 1948 (lost to Theodore F. Green). The 1948 race was the high-water mark of his political career after South Kingstown Town Council, RI State Senate, and an unsuccessful 1932 US House run.

As Liaison Officer to the Chief Quartermaster of the U.S. Army based in England from February 1944, he played a major role in food distribution to liberated northwestern Europe. Discharged Lt. Colonel November 1945 after Tunisia, Palermo, and Naples Civil Affairs assignments.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote a 1949 book on civil affairs reconstruction
narrative_blocks.body: Published 'Civil Affairs: An Experiment in Reconstruction' (1949) based on his WWII Liaison Officer experience. Also co-authored a 1951 American Fisheries Society paper on modification of the sexual cycle in brook trout — his family's American Fish Culture Company connection.

He enlisted Troop D 1st NY Cavalry as a private in October 1915 at twenty-three; called to active duty for the Mexican border intervention June 1916; commissioned 2nd Lt. then promoted 1st Lt. in the 14th U.S. Cavalry at Del Rio TX. Discharged February 1919 — never went overseas in WWI.

education.notes: — Wolf's Head Senior Society. Psi Upsilon Fraternity.
